title: Driven from New Orleans: How Nonprofits Betray Public Housing and Promote Privatization By John Arena. University of Minnesota Press. 2012. 344 pages. $27.50 paper
abstract: This is a fascinating and well-crafted study of political struggles over privatization of public housing in New Orleans. The author presents a no-holds barred class analysis of the derailment of direct action tactics by a coalition of real estate developers, members of the local black urban political regime, and leaders of both local and national nonprofit organizations bent on demolition of public housing. It is argued that identity politics and the politics of difference were mobilized in the nonprofit sector to undermine potential for a broad working class movement to more militantly challenge demolition and privatization of public housing. The book begins with the shift in government housing policy from support of large, publicly owned housing projects to support of public–private partnerships and mixed-use developments that combine subsidized with market rate and privately owned units. Supporters of the policy change like President Bill Clinton and sociologist William J. Wilson agree that the new urbanism model of dispersed, mixed income developments effectively addresses problems of social isolation and concentrated poverty in the public housing sector. The policy came to life in the Hope VI program authorized by the Housing and Community Development Act of 1992.
